title: Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous

items.question: I ___ three emails this morning.
explanations: 'Been writing' describes the activity, but 'three emails' is a finished quantity.
Correct! A completed number takes the present perfect simple: 'have written'.
'Write' is present simple (a habit), not this count up to now.
'Wrote' needs a finished time; 'this morning' isn't finished, so use the present perfect.

items.question: Sorry I'm late! How long ___ you ___?
explanations: Correct! 'How long' + the ongoing activity takes the continuous: 'have you been waiting'.
'Have you waited' focuses on a result; for the duration of the activity, use the continuous.
'Did you wait' asks about a finished time, not duration up to now.
'Are you waiting' is only about this moment.

items.question: Your clothes are covered in paint! You ___.
explanations: 'Have painted' focuses on a finished result, but the paint on your clothes points to the recent activity.
Correct! A recent activity with a visible result now takes the continuous: 'have been painting'.
'Painted' needs a finished time and doesn't link the activity to now.
'Paint' is present simple (a habit), not this recent activity.

items.question: Great news — I ___ my essay at last!
explanations: 'Finish' isn't used in the continuous to mean a completed result.
Correct! A finished result takes the present perfect simple: 'have finished'.
'Finish' is present simple, not this completed action linked to now.
'Was finishing' is past continuous, not a present result.

items.question: We ___ each other since we were children.
explanations: 'Know' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous.
Correct! State verbs take the present perfect simple: 'have known'.
'Know' is present simple; 'since we were children' links past to now, so use the present perfect.
'Knew' is past simple, but 'since…' connects to now.

items.question: He ___ ten kilometres today.
explanations: 'Been running' describes the activity, but 'ten kilometres' is a finished amount.
Correct! A completed quantity takes the present perfect simple: 'has run'.
'Runs' is present simple (a habit), not this count up to now.
'Ran' needs a finished time; 'today' isn't finished, so use the present perfect.

items.question: I'm exhausted — I ___ all day.
explanations: 'Have shopped' focuses on a result; here the point is the tiring activity across the day.
Correct! A long activity with a result now (exhaustion) takes the continuous: 'have been shopping'.
'Shop' is present simple (a habit), not this ongoing activity.
'Shopped' needs a finished time and doesn't link to now.

items.question: At last, the builders ___ the bridge.
explanations: 'Been building' describes the ongoing activity, but 'at last' points to the finished result.
Correct! A completed result takes the present perfect simple: 'have built'.
'Build' is present simple, not this completed action.
'Built' needs a finished time; the present perfect links it to now ('at last').

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I started waiting an hour ago and I'm still waiting.'
options: I've been waiting for an hour.
explanations: 'I've waited an hour' focuses on the result; the sentence stresses the ongoing activity.
Correct! An action continuing up to now, focusing on duration, takes the continuous: 'have been waiting'.
'Waited' (past simple) suggests the waiting has stopped, but you're still waiting.
'I wait for an hour' is a present habit, not this specific ongoing action.

items.question: Which sentence focuses on a finished number?
options: I've been reading books all day.
I've read four books this month.
explanations: 'Been reading all day' focuses on the activity, not a number.
Correct! 'Four books' is a completed quantity, which takes the present perfect simple.
'Been reading since lunch' focuses on duration, not a number.
'I read books at night' is a present habit.
